exemplar.title: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: The essay remains explicitly focused on the prescribed title, continually returning to the question of whether revered knowledge is more fragile than assumed.

Both required areas of knowledge (the arts and the natural sciences) are addressed with concrete, relevant examples (Casablanca, the Golden Ratio, spontaneous generation/Pasteur, and the James Webb Space Telescope).
Careful selection of modern and historical cases (e.g., JWST and Pasteurâs experiments) demonstrates engagement with the provisional nature of scientific knowledge.
The introduction uses Baconâs quote effectively to frame the discussion, and the conclusion reinforces the dynamic and provisional nature of knowledge across disciplines.
There is some awareness of alternative perspectives (Cubism challenging the Golden Ratio; potential future confirmations of JWST data).
weaknesses: Many links to the areas of knowledge remain superficial; assertions about subjectivity in art or provisionality in science are stated rather than deeply explored.
Arguments are sometimes unclear or loosely structured, with repetition and occasional logical jumps within paragraphs.
Evaluation of alternative viewpoints is minimalâstrengths and limitations are noted but not rigorously weighed or probed.
Key methodological and technical details are omitted (e.g., mathematical definition of Ï, Pasteurâs experimental controls), weakening analytical depth.
The central knowledge question is vague and never explicitly stated, undermining coherence and signposting.
Implications of the arguments are only briefly touched upon in the conclusion rather than being a sustained thread throughout the essay.
Colloquial phrasing and run-on sentences occasionally disrupt the academic tone and clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The strand assessment places this essay securely in Band 5â6: it stays on topic, offers identifiable arguments, and uses relevant examples, but it lacks the clarity, coherence and critical depth required to reach Band 7â8. Superficial treatment of key conceptual links, minimal evaluation of alternative perspectives and uneven argument structure limit its analytical rigour. These characteristics justify awarding 5 out of 10 for the essay as a whole. Because the official examinerâs overall mark of 5 is final, we assign the full 5 points to this criterion, reflecting the strengths and weaknesses identified in the strand assessment.

annotations.comment: The thesis statement introduces âfragilityâ but remains vague; consider articulating a precise knowledge question to guide the essay and clarify how âfragileâ will be defined and assessed.
The essay would benefit from explicitly stating the central knowledge question early on rather than implying it; this will help maintain focus and coherence throughout the argument.
The use of quotations from Degas and Albers introduces subjectivity well but lacks subsequent analysis; the student should explore how these perspectives complicate the notion of artistic truths.

The analysis of Casablanca remains largely descriptive; the student should delve deeper into why shifts in interpretation reveal the fragility of artistic knowledge rather than merely recounting its reception history.
The ratio is not defined mathematically in the essay; including $$\phi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}$$ would anchor the discussion and demonstrate precise understanding.

The student does not explain key aspects of Pasteurâs experimental design (e.g., use of controls and swanâneck flasks); elaborating on methodology would strengthen analysis of how the theory was overturned.
Overall, the essay would benefit from clearer signposting of arguments and tighter integration of analysis with TOK concepts such as perspective, evidence, and methodology.
The phrase âHOW fragile comes inâ is imprecise and interrupts coherence; the student should rephrase to explicitly link the Cubist challenge to the fragility of revered artistic knowledge.
The argument that Cubism destabilizes the Golden Ratio is asserted rather than analyzed; the student should explain how cultural shifts directly undermine the ratioâs perceived universality.
The discussion of spontaneous generation and Pasteurâs experiments is well chosen to illustrate the falsifiability and provisional nature of scientific knowledge.
Limited consideration is given to the broader implications of falsification for scientific practice; the student should discuss what this case reveals about how knowledge claims endure or fail.
The analysis of JWST remains at a general level; the student should provide specific instances where its data have already challenged existing cosmological models to illustrate fragility.

The student lists usage by da Vinci and Michelangelo but does not critically examine when or why the Golden Ratioâs status was subsequently challenged; deeper evaluation of historical critiques is needed.
Referencing Cubism as a movement that opposed classical proportionality effectively introduces an alternative perspective that tests the solidity of established aesthetic principles.
Selecting the JWST as a modern science example demonstrates engagement with current developments and highlights the temporal dimension of what is considered âreveredâ in science.
Repetitive references to technological advancement dilute the argument; consider consolidating these points and focusing on one or two pivotal implications for the telescopeâs revered status.
The conclusion correctly identifies fragility as both a limitation and a strength, reinforcing the dynamic and provisional nature of knowledge across disciplines.
The colloquial phrasing âyes, our most revered knowledge is more fragileâ undermines formal tone; reword this to maintain academic register and reinforce the essayâs sophistication.

The introduction effectively invokes Baconâs quote to ground the discussion on reliability and authenticity, providing a strong contextual basis for examining the fragility of revered knowledge in both the arts and the sciences.
Consider adding a brief roadmap or signposting at the end of the introduction to outline how each example will develop your argument about the fragility of revered knowledge.

The statement that the essay will explore âCasablanca,â the Golden Ratio, spontaneous generation and the JWST demonstrates clear awareness of the requirement to address two areas of knowledge with concrete examples.
The phrase âFollowing on First examplesâ appears unfinished and weakens the transition; refine this to clearly introduce the upcoming case study in the arts section.
Selecting Casablanca as an example effectively demonstrates how cultural and historical contexts can elevate a workâs revered status over time, illustrating the fragility of artistic reputation.
Several sentences are overly long and run-on, which obscures the argument; breaking these into focused statements will improve clarity and strengthen the logical flow.
The recognition that the filmâs meaning and value depend on audience interpretation underscores the contingent nature of revered artistic knowledge and its inherent vulnerability.
Introducing the Golden Ratio as a second arts example shows awareness of the need for diverse case studies and establishes a mathematical dimension to aesthetic knowledge.
The conclusion would be more powerful if it revisited the alternative perspectives introduced earlier (e.g., Cubism, positive potential of JWST), synthesizing them rather than stating generalities.
The final paragraph effectively underscores that reverence is not static but an ongoing dialogue, neatly tying together examples from both arts and sciences.

The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
